pi_items.source: Project Gutenberg, Amazon, Wikipedia, and multiple library catalogs confirm this is the correct title of Holmes' 1881 work
Library of Congress and all authoritative sources confirm Oliver Wendell Holmes Jr. as the author of The Common Law (1881)
Wikipedia, Britannica, and Library of Congress all confirm the full first names as Oliver Wendell Holmes Jr.
Multiple sources including Wikipedia, Project Gutenberg, and Britannica confirm Leviathan is the correct title of Hobbes' 1651 work
Britannica and all academic sources confirm Thomas Hobbes (1588-1679) as the author of Leviathan published in 1651
Wikipedia and Britannica confirm Thomas Hobbes is the correct full author name
Wikipedia, Britannica, Library of Congress, and Constitution Center confirm this is the correct English title of Montesquieu's 1748 work (original French: De l'esprit des lois)

Library of Congress authority record (id.loc.gov/authorities/names/n79063793.html) shows 'Montesquieu, Charles de Secondat, baron de, 1689-1755'. Montesquieu is the commonly used name, though his birth surname is Secondat

Library of Congress authority record authorizes the full name as 'Charles Louis de Secondat' or 'Charles de Secondat'. Multiple academic sources (Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, Online Library of Liberty, Britannica) identify him as Charles-Louis de Secondat or Charles Louis de Secondat. The metadata claim of just 'Charles' is incomplete.

privileged_info_summary: The task fixes author names in a Juris-M legal database. Three classic legal texts are involved: (1) The Common Law by Oliver Wendell Holmes, published 1881, author names verified as Oliver Wendell Holmes; (2) Leviathan by Thomas Hobbes, published 1651, author names verified as Thomas Hobbes; (3) The Spirit of Laws by Montesquieu, published 1748, author name verified as Charles-Louis de Secondat (known as Montesquieu). The metadata claim that Montesquieu's first name is simply 'Charles' is incomplete—the Library of Congress authority record establishes it as 'Charles-Louis de Secondat' or 'Charles Louis de Secondat'. The task data is synthetic, injected into the database by the setup script with deliberate ALL CAPS formatting for correction.
